figures.caption: Table 1: FID scores (↓) on CIFAR10 32 × 32, FFHQ 64 × 64, AFHQv2 64 × 64 and ImageNet 64 × 64. 𝑁 is the number of subnetworks (28 in our experiments) while we show 7 subnetworks here for space limits. The training overhead of our OFA network remains constant as the number of compressed subnetworks increases. “+Fine-tune" denotes the specific subnetworks are further fine-tuned after grabbing weights from our OFA network. † are the performance of pre-trained models. We mark the best results under each P𝑖 in bold.

Figure 3: Comparison between our proposed subnetwork construction approach and other ablations. For simplicity, “Arch” and “Imp” denote the “Architecture” and “Importance”, respectively. All OFA networks are under the same training and evaluation settings. The results are obtained under U-Net, all the FID scores of 28 subnetworks are plotted. We also report the averaged FID across all subnetworks for each method. We can observe that our method consistently achieves a better averaged FID on all datasets.

Figure 6: Overview of our OFA-Diffusion Compression framework. On the left, we present the structure of U-Net architecture. On the right, we show the details of layer in blocks and the compressed modules (𝐶𝑜𝑛𝑣1, 𝐶𝑜𝑛𝑣2 and Self-Attention) in our compression framework. We omit GroupNorm and time embedding for simplicity.Given a retention rate P𝑖 , only the top-P𝑖 important parameters will be preserved in each block, then these budgets will be unevenly allocated to each layer in a greedy manner. By doing so, for two retention rates P𝑖 < P𝑗 , in each layer, the corresponded module of P𝑖 is a subset of the module corresponded to P𝑗 .
Figure 12: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on ImageNet 64 × 64 (EDM conditional sampling, U-Net backbone).
Figure 11: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on AFHQv2 64 × 64 (EDM unconditional sampling).
Figure 9: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on CIFAR10 32 × 32 (EDM unconditional sampling, U-Net backbone).
Figure 10: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on FFHQ 64 × 64 (EDM unconditional sampling, U-Net backbone).

Figure 1: Overview of our OFA-Diffusion Compression framework. On the left, we present the structure of U-ViT architecture. On the right, we show the details of layers in blocks (FFN and Self-Attention) in our compression framework. We omit GroupNorm and time embedding for simplicity. Given a retention rate P𝑖 , only the top-P𝑖 important parameters will be preserved in each block, then these budgets will be unevenly allocated to each layer in a greedy manner. By doing so, for two retention rates P𝑖 < P𝑗 , in each layer, the corresponded module of P𝑖 is a subset of the module corresponded to P𝑗 .
Table 3: Ablation of training strategy in OFA-Diffusion Compression framework evaluated on U-Net. The best results for each P𝑖 are bold marked.
Table 2: FID scores (↓) on CIFAR10 32 × 32 and CelebA 64 × 64 with a U-ViT backbone, and on MS-COCO2014 512 × 512 with a Stable Diffusion v1.5 backbone.
Figure 15: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on MS-COCO 512× 512 (Stable Diffusion text-to-image generation).

Figure 5: Comparison of FID versus MACs with different reweighting ratios on CIFAR10 under U-Net.𝑚 → 1.0 means 𝑤P1 =𝑚𝑤P𝑁 . We also report the averaged FID over subnetworks. For efficiency, we evaluate FID on 10K generated samples.
Figure 14: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on CelebA 64 × 64 (U-ViT unconditional sampling, Transformer backbone).
Figure 13: Samples from subnetworks trained by different approaches with the same random seed on cifar10 32 × 32 (U-ViT unconditional sampling, Transformer backbone).
